h1Title: Chronic Migraine Pain Management: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic migraine is defined as 15 or more headache days per month for at least three consecutive months, with at least eight of those days meeting migraine criteria. Approximately 1.4-2.2% of the global population lives with chronic migraine, corresponding to roughly 100-150 million people worldwide, making it one of the most disabling neurological conditions by years lived with disability (GBD 2021). It evolves from episodic migraine — typically via medication overuse, sleep disruption, stress, or hormonal factors — and is distinguished by its frequency and the disability it causes. Three classes of preventive therapy — topiramate, amitriptyline, and anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies — reduce monthly headache days by 30-50%, and onabotulinumtoxin A (Botox) reduces headache days by approximately four days per month in RCTs.

keyStats.prevalence: 1.4-2.2% of the global population; roughly 3.5% of US adults meet diagnostic criteria for chronic migraine at any point (Diamond et al. 2007)
keyStats.demographics: Women are 3× more affected than men; peak prevalence age 35-45; chronic migraine is twice as common in individuals with low household income
keyStats.avgAge: Most patients with chronic migraine have had episodic migraine since their teens or twenties before chronification occurs, typically in their 30s-40s
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100-150 million people with chronic migraine worldwide; migraine (all forms) is the second leading cause of disability globally (GBD 2021)

definition: Chronic migraine (ICD-10: G43.7) is a headache disorder defined by the International Headache Society ICHD-3 criteria as 15 or more headache days per month for more than 3 months, with at least 8 days per month fulfilling migraine criteria (with or without aura) or responding to migraine-specific treatment. The underlying pathophysiology involves sensitisation of the trigeminovascular system — the trigeminal nerve fibres innervating meningeal blood vessels — and dysregulation of calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), a potent vasodilatory and pro-nociceptive neuropeptide. During chronification, central sensitisation develops, reducing the threshold for headache initiation and sustaining pain through neuroplastic changes in the trigeminal nucleus caudalis and cortex. Medication overuse headache (MOH), a significant complication, occurs when acute headache medications are used on more than 10-15 days per month for more than 3 months — this paradoxically worsens headache frequency through CGRP upregulation and central sensitisation. Chronic migraine is managed by neurologists specialising in headache medicine, pain medicine specialists, and increasingly by trained primary care clinicians using CGRP-pathway therapies.

typesClassification.description: Headache meeting migraine criteria on 8+ days per month without associated transient neurological symptoms. The most common form. Characterised by unilateral or bilateral moderate-to-severe headache with nausea, photo- and phonophobia.

Recurrent transient focal neurological disturbances (visual scintillations, hemianopia, unilateral sensory loss, or aphasia) preceding or accompanying headache. Aura episodes typically last 20-60 minutes. Associated with modestly elevated stroke risk, particularly in women who smoke or use combined oral contraceptives.

typesClassification.type: Chronic migraine with medication overuse headache (MOH)
typesClassification.description: Superimposed pattern of daily or near-daily headache from overuse of triptans (>10 days/month), analgesics, or opioids. MOH is present in 50-80% of patients presenting to headache clinics with chronic migraine. Withdrawal of the overused medication is an essential component of treatment.

Attacks temporally linked to the menstrual cycle — within 2 days before or 3 days after menstruation onset — occurring on most cycles. Driven by oestrogen withdrawal. Often more severe, longer, and less responsive to standard acute therapy. Preventive hormonal or triptanspecific strategies are required.

primarySymptoms: Headache on 15 or more days per month, with attacks lasting 4-72 hours if untreated, often unilateral, pulsating, moderate to severe in intensity.
Nausea occurring in approximately 80% of migraine attacks, with vomiting in 30%, severely limiting oral medication effectiveness and daily function.
Photophobia and phonophobia — intolerance of normal ambient light and sound — that forces the patient into a dark, quiet room during attacks.
Cutaneous allodynia in 60-70% of patients with chronic migraine — touch of skin, hair, or clothing on the scalp and face that is normally painless becomes acutely painful during attacks.
Prodromal symptoms beginning 6-24 hours before headache onset: yawning, neck stiffness, food cravings, mood change, and fatigue — provide a diagnostic window for early triptan intervention.
Cognitive dysfunction during and between attacks — slowed processing, word-finding difficulties, and reduced memory consolidation, sometimes called migraine-associated cognitive impairment.
Sleep disruption: difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, frequent waking with headache, or headache triggering on awakening — both a symptom and a driver of chronification.
Neck pain and cervicogenic-appearing discomfort — present in 75% of migraine attacks; driven by trigeminal-cervical nucleus convergence rather than primary cervical pathology.
Dizziness and vestibular migraine symptoms in a subset: vertigo lasting minutes to hours with or without concurrent headache in people with a migraine history.
Psychiatric comorbidity symptoms — prominent depression and anxiety worsen headache frequency independently of treatment through shared neurobiological pathways.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive increase in monthly headache days from episodic to subacute frequency (8-14 days per month) — the critical transition window for preventive intervention before chronification.
Increasing reliance on acute headache medications more than twice weekly — the primary risk factor for medication overuse headache, which accelerates chronification.
New or worsening sleep disorder, anxiety, or depression alongside increasing headache frequency — modifiable risk factors for chronification that require concurrent management.
emergencySigns: Thunderclap headache — sudden severe headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds to a minute — requires immediate CT head and lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage; migraine never presents this way for the first time.
Headache with fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia — requires immediate assessment for bacterial meningitis; do not attribute to migraine without excluding infection.
Migraine with aura lasting more than 60 minutes or producing new neurological deficit — requires urgent MRI and neurology review to exclude ischaemic stroke, particularly in women over 40.

causes.description: Calcitonin gene-related peptide is a neuropeptide released from trigeminal afferent terminals that causes meningeal vasodilation, mast cell degranulation, and central pain sensitisation. Plasma CGRP is elevated during migraine attacks and during the interictal period in chronic migraine. Blocking the CGRP receptor or ligand is the mechanistic basis of the most effective modern preventive therapies.

Using acute headache medications on more than 10-15 days per month causes upregulation of CGRP, serotonin receptor downregulation, and cortical spreading depression facilitation — all of which increase attack frequency. MOH is present in 50-80% of patients referred to specialist headache clinics.

causes.cause: Central sensitisation from repeated migraine attacks
causes.description: Each untreated or undertreated migraine attack leaves a neuroplastic footprint in the trigeminal nucleus and cortex. Over months to years this lowers the pain initiation threshold, allowing more triggers — and eventually no external trigger — to precipitate attacks.

Chronic sleep insufficiency (less than 6 hours per night), erratic sleep-wake cycles, and sleep disorders including obstructive sleep apnoea are independently associated with transition from episodic to chronic migraine. Sleep deprivation lowers cortical excitability thresholds and elevates CGRP.

Oestrogen withdrawal at menstruation, perimenopause, and postpartum drives migraine attacks through hypothalamic modulation of the trigeminovascular axis. Women are 3× more likely than men to develop chronic migraine, primarily due to this hormonal mechanism.

causes.cause: Psychological stress and comorbid psychiatric conditions
causes.description: Chronic anxiety and depression are present in 30-50% of chronic migraine patients and independently drive chronification through shared hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation and central sensitisation. Treating mood disorders improves headache outcomes independent of direct analgesic action.

riskFactors.factor: Episodic migraine with high attack frequency (>4/month)
riskFactors.description: The strongest single predictor of chronification — patients with 10-14 headache days per month have a 10-fold higher risk of becoming chronic compared with those with 1-4 days per month.
Medication overuse is the most modifiable risk factor for chronification. Withdrawal of overused medication reverses MOH-driven frequency increase in 50-70% of patients within 2-3 months.
Women have 3× the chronic migraine prevalence of men, driven primarily by hormonal mechanisms and higher rates of anxiety and depression — themselves independent risk factors.
BMI ≥30 is associated with a 3-fold higher risk of migraine chronification. Weight loss of 5% or more is associated with reduction in headache frequency in intervention studies.
Comorbid depression triples the risk of episodic-to-chronic migraine conversion. Treating depression with SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) has dual benefit for mood and headache prevention.

riskFactors.factor: Poor sleep quality or less than 6 hours per night
riskFactors.description: Sleep disorders are present in 50-80% of chronic migraine patients. Sleep apnoea, when treated, reduces headache frequency substantially in observational studies.
Chronic migraine is twice as prevalent in the lowest income quintile. Limited access to preventive treatment, stress burden, and irregular sleep-work patterns all contribute.

affectedDemographics: Women are three times more affected than men; peak prevalence in women aged 35-45 years (AMPP Study, Buse et al. 2012).
Low-income adults have twice the chronic migraine prevalence of high-income groups — access to CGRP monoclonal antibodies is particularly limited by cost in low- and middle-income settings.
Chronic migraine causes 11 million missed workdays per year in the United States alone; 90% of sufferers report some disability during attacks.
Paediatric chronic migraine (defined on the same ICHD-3 frequency criteria) affects approximately 0.4% of children aged 12-17 in population surveys.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows ICHD-3 criteria: 15 or more headache days per month for more than 3 months, with at least 8 days meeting migraine criteria, in the absence of another causative diagnosis. A detailed headache diary tracking frequency, duration, character, associated symptoms, and medication use over 4 weeks is essential — many patients underestimate frequency. The clinical history must identify medication overuse: use of triptans, ergotamines, analgesics (NSAIDs, paracetamol), or opioids on more than 10-15 days per month for 3+ months. Neurological examination is expected to be normal in primary chronic migraine; any abnormality mandates MRI. Red flags requiring neuroimaging include: thunderclap onset, progressive headache over weeks to months, headache triggered by cough or Valsalva, new headache after age 50, fever with headache, or neurological symptoms lasting more than 60 minutes. Once secondary causes are excluded, the history establishes migraine subtype, comorbidities (mood disorders, sleep disorders, cardiovascular risk), and prior treatment response to guide preventive agent selection. The AAN-AHS (2012) and EHF/EFNS (2022) guidelines provide validated diagnostic and management pathways.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies headache days per month, attack duration, character, associated symptoms, and acute medication use; essential for ICHD-3 diagnosis and for detecting medication overuse
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient records each headache day, intensity, and medications taken in a paper or app-based diary. Takes 30-60 seconds per entry. The treating clinician reviews at the next appointment to confirm the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies any focal deficits or signs of secondary headache; expected to be normal in primary chronic migraine
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside examination of eye movements, facial sensation, reflexes, coordination, and gait. Takes 10-15 minutes at the GP or neurology appointment.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with and without gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes secondary causes — intracranial mass, Arnold-Chiari malformation, venous sinus thrombosis, or leptomeningeal disease — when red flags are present or diagnosis is uncertain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. No radiation. Contrast (gadolinium) injected via hand vein if enhancement is needed. Normal in uncomplicated chronic migraine.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for comorbid depression and anxiety, which occur in 30-50% of chronic migraine patients and are independent drivers of chronification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two brief questionnaires taking 5 minutes combined. Results guide whether antidepressant medication or psychological referral is added to the migraine management plan.

diagnosticTests.test: Validated headache disability scale (MIDAS or HIT-6)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies functional impairment from headache to guide treatment intensity and monitor response to preventive therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MIDAS (Migraine Disability Assessment) or HIT-6 (Headache Impact Test-6): 5-6 questions, 2-3 minutes. Scores classify disability as mild, moderate, or severe and are tracked across treatment intervals.

treatmentOverview: Management of chronic migraine requires both acute treatment optimisation and preventive therapy. Acute treatment: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, zolmitriptan) remain first-line for moderate-to-severe migraine attacks but must be limited to no more than 9-10 treatment days per month to avoid MOH. Gepants (rimegepant, ubrogepant) and lasmiditan are newer acute agents that can be used more frequently without MOH risk. For attacks with vomiting, non-oral routes (nasal, subcutaneous, or rectal) are used. Preventive therapy is indicated in chronic migraine by definition and should reduce monthly headache days by at least 30-50%. The AAN-AHS 2012 guideline and the EHF 2022 consensus support the following preventive agents: topiramate (25-100 mg/day) and valproate are level-A anticonvulsants for prevention; amitriptyline (10-75 mg at night) is evidence-based for chronic migraine. Beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol) are first-line for episodic migraine but have modest evidence specifically in chronic migraine. CGRP pathway-targeted therapies represent the most significant advance in decades: onabotulinumtoxin A (Botox, 155-195 units injected to 31-39 scalp and neck sites every 12 weeks) is approved for chronic migraine and reduces monthly headache days by approximately 4 days per month compared with placebo (PREEMPT trials). Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies — erenumab (70-140 mg monthly SC), fremanezumab (225 mg monthly or 675 mg quarterly SC), galcanezumab (120 mg monthly SC), and eptinezumab (100-300 mg IV quarterly) — reduce monthly migraine days by 30-50% at 12 weeks and have superior tolerability profiles compared with oral preventives. Medication overuse withdrawal is mandatory before assessing response to preventive therapy: abrupt or structured withdrawal of the overused agent with bridging therapy (naproxen, prednisolone) reduces headache frequency by 3-5 days per month within 2-3 months in 50-70% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab)
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) or intravenous (eptinezumab) monoclonal antibodies that block either the CGRP ligand or its receptor. Monthly or quarterly dosing. First-line preventive in patients who have failed 2+ oral preventives.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-50% reduction in monthly migraine days versus placebo at 12 weeks; 30-50% responder rates in phase-3 trials; superior tolerability to topiramate

medicalTreatments.name: OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox, 155-195 units IM every 12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: 31-39 injections to scalp, neck, and shoulder muscle points every 3 months. Mechanism involves blocking peripheral CGRP release from trigeminal terminals. Approved for chronic migraine only (not episodic). Administered in headache or pain clinic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 4 fewer monthly headache days versus placebo at 24 weeks (PREEMPT 1+2 meta-analysis); response improves over multiple treatment cycles

medicalTreatments.name: Topiramate (25-100 mg/day, dose escalated weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Anticonvulsant with multiple mechanisms including sodium channel blockade, glutamate antagonism, and carbonic anhydrase inhibition. AAN level-A evidence for migraine prevention. Cognitive side-effects (word-finding difficulty) and weight loss are the most notable effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 28-36% reduction in monthly migraine days versus placebo at 26 weeks; response rate 50% at 100 mg/day in the pivotal MIGR-002 trial

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline (10-75 mg at night)
medicalTreatments.description: Tricyclic antidepressant with serotonin and noradrenaline reuptake inhibition and sodium channel blockade. Used at lower doses for headache prevention than for depression. Particularly useful when comorbid insomnia or depression is present.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Level-B AAN evidence for migraine prevention; approximately 25-30% reduction in headache frequency in meta-analyses; often combined with topiramate for additive effect

medicalTreatments.name: Gepants — acute therapy (rimegepant 75 mg, ubrogepant 50-100 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Small-molecule CGRP receptor antagonists for acute treatment of migraine attacks. Can be used on more headache days than triptans without MOH risk. Rimegepant is also approved for episodic migraine prevention (every-other-day dosing).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain freedom at 2 hours in 19-21% versus 11-13% for placebo; non-inferior to triptans with lower cardiovascular risk and no MOH liability

medicalTreatments.name: MOH withdrawal with bridging therapy (naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 4-8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured withdrawal of the overused acute medication with bridging NSAID and short-course steroid (prednisolone 60 mg tapering over 5 days). Reduces chronification substrate in 50-70% of patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Headache frequency reduction of 3-5 days per month at 3 months post-withdrawal; essential prerequisite to assess preventive therapy response

surgicalOptions.name: Greater occipital nerve (GON) block
surgicalOptions.description: Injection of local anaesthetic with or without steroid at the greater occipital nerve. Provides temporary preventive benefit and can bridge to effective oral or injectable preventive therapy. Evidence supports short-term benefit; not a permanent solution.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 50-60% of patients achieve significant headache reduction for 4-12 weeks after a single injection; repeat injections effective; low procedural risk

alternativeTreatments.name: Riboflavin (vitamin B2, 400 mg/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Level-B AAN evidence for migraine prevention. Reduces migraine frequency by approximately 2 days per month versus placebo in a pivotal RCT. Safe, inexpensive, and useful as adjunct or for patients declining pharmacological prevention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) for migraine
alternativeTreatments.description: Adapted MBCT programme for chronic headache patients reduces headache frequency and disability through reductions in pain catastrophising and central sensitisation. RCT evidence supports modest headache-specific benefit alongside depression and anxiety improvement.

whySeeSpecialist: Neurology or headache specialist assessment is indicated when chronic migraine fails two standard preventive agents, when anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies or Botox are being considered, when MOH requires supervised withdrawal, or when aura symptoms are atypical. Pain medicine or palliative care specialists address chronic migraine in the context of broader chronic pain comorbidity.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurologist with a subspecialty interest in headache medicine, or a pain medicine specialist familiar with CGRP-pathway therapies. For Botox treatment, verify the prescriber performs at least 20-30 chronic migraine Botox procedures per year. Access to a multidisciplinary headache service (neurologist, psychologist, physiotherapist, specialist nurse) improves outcomes compared with single-clinician management.
hospitalCriteria: Access to onabotulinumtoxin A (Botox) treatment for chronic migraine under specialist supervision
Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody prescribing and monitoring capability
Psychologist or CBT practitioner within the headache service for comorbid depression and anxiety
MOH withdrawal protocol with inpatient or day-case bridging if outpatient withdrawal fails
MRI capability for red-flag headache assessment within 48 hours
keyFacilities: Tertiary headache centres with combined neurology and pain medicine expertise
Outpatient headache clinics at neurology or pain medicine departments
Community neurology services with CGRP-pathway prescribing capacity
preventionStrategies: Start preventive therapy when headache days reach 8 or more per month — before chronification is established.
Limit acute medication use to no more than 9-10 days per month regardless of medication type to prevent MOH.
Maintain regular sleep of 7-8 hours per night — irregular sleep schedules are among the most consistent and modifiable triggers for migraine chronification.
Manage comorbid depression or anxiety with evidence-based treatment — SNRIs such as venlafaxine provide dual benefit for both conditions.
Avoid obesity — weight management is associated with a significant reduction in migraine frequency in multiple intervention studies.
Reduce caffeine intake to less than 200 mg/day — caffeine withdrawal is a migraine trigger, and chronic overconsumption contributes to MOH in susceptible individuals.
lifestyleModifications: Keep a headache diary tracking frequency, triggers, medication use, sleep, and menstrual cycle to identify personal triggers and demonstrate preventive therapy response.
Maintain a regular sleep schedule, including weekends — variable wake times are a potent chronification driver.
Eat regular meals and maintain adequate hydration — meal skipping and dehydration are among the most common identifiable attack triggers.
Exercise regularly at moderate intensity — 3× weekly aerobic exercise reduces migraine frequency by approximately 20% in an RCT (Varkey et al. 2011).
Manage screen time and bright light exposure, particularly in the evening — photosensitisation is both a symptom and a trigger in chronic migraine.
Address occupational stress with structured relaxation, workplace adjustments, and if necessary psychological support.

recommended: Magnesium-rich foods (dark leafy greens, nuts, legumes, whole grains) — magnesium deficiency is associated with higher migraine frequency; supplementation (400 mg/day) has level-B evidence
Regular meals with low-glycaemic-index carbohydrates to maintain stable blood glucose and avoid hunger-triggered attacks
Adequate hydration — 1.5-2 L of water per day; dehydration is a common precipitant
avoid: Skipping meals — hunger is a highly consistent personal migraine trigger in the majority of patients
Excess alcohol, particularly red wine (contains tyramine) and champagne — direct vasodilatory and CGRP-releasing triggers
High caffeine intake (>200 mg/day) — chronic use followed by withdrawal is a common trigger; gradual reduction advised rather than abrupt cessation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity aerobic exercise three times per week — 40 minutes per session — reduces monthly migraine frequency by approximately 2 days versus no exercise in a randomised trial. Exercise is comparable to topiramate for reducing attack frequency with a superior tolerability profile. High-intensity exercise can trigger attacks in some patients — a consistent warm-up and pre-exercise hydration reduce this risk.
dailyManagement: Take preventive medication at the prescribed dose every day, not only on headache days — preventive efficacy depends on continuous pharmacological presence.
Treat migraine attacks early at the first sign of pain — waiting until pain is severe significantly reduces triptan efficacy.
Record each headache day, medication taken, and pain response in the diary — this data drives prescribing decisions at every clinic visit.
Protect sleep with a regular bedtime and morning rising time — the single most impactful behavioural intervention for chronic migraine.
Monitor monthly headache days every 4 weeks — if not decreasing by month 3 on preventive therapy, review and escalate treatment.
prognosis: With systematic preventive treatment, approximately 50-60% of patients with chronic migraine achieve a 30-50% reduction in monthly headache days within 3-6 months. Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies produce sustained response in approximately 50% of patients at 12 months, with a meaningful proportion achieving fewer than 4 headache days per month — effectively reverting to episodic migraine. Spontaneous remission of chronic migraine — reversion to episodic migraine without treatment — occurs in approximately 26% of patients per year in observational studies. Factors predicting sustained chronicity include persistent medication overuse, untreated depression, poor sleep, obesity, and ongoing psychosocial stress. Long-term outcomes are significantly worse in patients with MOH who do not complete withdrawal: MOH prevents preventive therapy from working and must be addressed before response can be assessed. After successful MOH withdrawal and prevention, relapse of MOH occurs in approximately 30% of patients within 2 years, requiring ongoing monitoring.
recoveryTimeline: MOH withdrawal effect: 2-3 months for headache frequency to decline. Preventive medication response: 8-12 weeks to assess topiramate or amitriptyline; 12 weeks for anti-CGRP antibodies and Botox. Full remission from chronic to episodic migraine: 6-18 months with effective preventive therapy and lifestyle change. Relapse prevention requires ongoing management for most patients.
complications: Medication overuse headache — affects 50-80% of specialist-referred chronic migraine patients; prevents preventive therapy from working and requires structured withdrawal.
Psychiatric comorbidity: depression and anxiety occur in 30-50% of patients and are both consequences and drivers of chronic migraine; require concurrent management.
Cervicogenic pain and musculoskeletal dysfunction from repeated attacks requiring supine recovery in poor positions — physiotherapy addresses secondary musculoskeletal sequelae.
Significant occupational disability — 90% of sufferers report some disability during attacks; 30% lose one or more workdays per attack, totalling 11 million lost workdays per year in the US.
Opioid dependence from inappropriate prescribing of opioids for migraine in primary care — opioids are not indicated for migraine management and accelerate chronification.

supportResources.name: American Migraine Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient-facing foundation providing evidence-based information on chronic migraine, treatment options, and specialist finder.

supportResources.name: Migraine Trust (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing specialist information, helpline, and advocacy for people with migraine and chronic headache.

supportResources.name: NICE NG150 — Headaches in over 12s: diagnosis and management
supportResources.description: UK evidence-based guideline on headache assessment, migraine diagnosis, and preventive treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Episodic migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Episodic migraine has fewer than 15 headache days per month. Chronic migraine is defined by 15 or more headache days per month for 3+ months with at least 8 migraine days. The distinction drives preventive treatment intensity — chronic migraine invariably requires preventive therapy; episodic migraine may not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread body pain with cognitive symptoms, fatigue, and sleep disruption. Chronic migraine predominantly produces episodic head pain with nausea and light sensitivity. Both involve central sensitisation and comorbid depression, and the two conditions co-occur at higher-than-chance rates, complicating management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trigeminal neuralgia produces brief (seconds to minutes), severe, electric-shock-like facial pain triggered by specific stimuli (chewing, talking). Chronic migraine causes longer headache episodes with nausea and autonomic features. Trigeminal neuralgia responds to carbamazepine; migraine does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic pain syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic pain syndrome is a biopsychosocial diagnosis encompassing widespread or multi-site pain with psychological amplification. Chronic migraine is a specific neurological headache disorder with ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria, localised primarily to head pain, and managed with specific anti-CGRP and preventive therapies.

relatedConditions.name: Episodic migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Episodic migraine is the precursor condition from which chronic migraine evolves; management shares pharmacological tools but preventive therapy is always indicated in chronic migraine.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Central sensitisation in chronic migraine shares mechanistic and treatment overlaps with neuropathic pain syndromes.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Fibromyalgia co-occurs with chronic migraine at significantly elevated rates; shared central sensitisation pathways are implicated.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve the trigeminal system; trigeminal neuralgia is the key clinical differential for facial pain that could be misdiagnosed as atypical migraine.

faqs.question: What is chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: Chronic migraine is defined as 15 or more headache days per month for at least 3 months, with at least 8 of those days meeting migraine criteria. It affects approximately 1.4-2.2% of the population globally and is one of the most disabling neurological conditions. It requires preventive medication rather than only acute treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between episodic and chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: Episodic migraine has fewer than 15 headache days per month. Chronic migraine has 15 or more headache days per month for at least 3 months. Chronic migraine requires preventive therapy to reduce frequency. It often evolves from episodic migraine through medication overuse, sleep disruption, or untreated comorbidities.

faqs.question: What is medication overuse headache?
faqs.answer: Medication overuse headache (MOH) occurs when acute headache medications — triptans, NSAIDs, opioids — are used on more than 10-15 days per month for 3 months. This paradoxically increases headache frequency. Withdrawal of the overused medication reduces headache frequency in 50-70% of patients within 2-3 months.

faqs.question: What are the best treatments for chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: First-line preventive treatments include anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab), onabotulinumtoxin A (Botox) injections every 12 weeks, and oral preventives topiramate or amitriptyline. MOH withdrawal is required before preventive therapy response can be assessed.

faqs.question: How does Botox treat chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) is injected into 31-39 scalp, neck, and shoulder muscle points every 12 weeks. It blocks peripheral release of CGRP from trigeminal nerve terminals, reducing migraine initiation. It reduces monthly headache days by approximately 4 compared with placebo and is approved specifically for chronic migraine.

faqs.question: What are CGRP drugs for migraine?
faqs.answer: CGRP drugs target calcitonin gene-related peptide, a key neuropeptide in migraine pathophysiology. Preventive CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) are injected monthly or quarterly. Gepants (rimegepant, ubrogepant) block the CGRP receptor acutely and can be used without triggering medication overuse headache.

faqs.question: How is chronic migraine diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Chronic migraine is diagnosed by ICHD-3 criteria: 15 or more headache days per month for 3 months with at least 8 migraine-quality days. A 4-week headache diary, neurological examination, and exclusion of secondary causes are required. MRI is performed when red flags are present.

faqs.question: What triggers can worsen chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: The most consistent worsening factors are medication overuse, irregular sleep, skipped meals, dehydration, high stress, bright light, menstrual cycle hormonal changes, and alcohol. Identifying and addressing personal triggers alongside preventive medication is essential to reducing monthly headache frequency.

faqs.question: Can chronic migraine be cured?
faqs.answer: Chronic migraine cannot always be permanently cured but can be effectively managed. Approximately 26% of patients revert spontaneously to episodic migraine per year. Anti-CGRP therapies achieve reversion to episodic migraine (fewer than 15 headache days per month) in approximately 50% of patients at 12 months of treatment.

faqs.question: Is chronic migraine linked to depression and anxiety?
faqs.answer: Yes. Depression and anxiety each occur in 30-50% of chronic migraine patients. Both are independent drivers of migraine chronification through shared hypothalamic and central sensitisation pathways. Treating comorbid depression with an SNRI such as venlafaxine provides dual benefit for mood and headache frequency.

faqs.question: Does exercise help chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Three sessions of 40-minute moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week reduces monthly migraine frequency by approximately 2 days in a randomised trial, comparable to topiramate. Exercise reduces CGRP sensitisation and improves sleep and mood — three pathways relevant to chronic migraine. Adequate warm-up and hydration reduce exercise-triggered attacks.

faqs.question: What role does sleep play in chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: Poor sleep quality and fewer than 6 hours per night are independent risk factors for migraine chronification. Sleep disorders affect 50-80% of chronic migraine patients. Maintaining a regular sleep schedule — same bedtime and waking time daily — is the single most impactful lifestyle change for reducing chronic migraine frequency.

faqs.question: Can opioids be used for chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: No. Opioids are not recommended for migraine management. They do not target migraine pathophysiology, have high MOH liability, and accelerate chronification. Current guidelines (AAN, EFNS, NICE) explicitly advise against opioid use for migraine. Patients receiving opioids for migraine require a structured withdrawal and transition to appropriate therapy.

faqs.question: What is the PREEMPT protocol for Botox in chronic migraine?
faqs.answer: The PREEMPT protocol is a standardised injection protocol for onabotulinumtoxinA in chronic migraine — 155-195 units injected across 31-39 fixed scalp, neck, and shoulder sites every 12 weeks. It was developed during the Phase-3 PREEMPT trials and is the basis for licensed Botox prescribing in chronic migraine.

faqs.question: Can chronic migraine affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic migraine affects approximately 0.4% of adolescents aged 12-17 using the same ICHD-3 frequency criteria as adults. Children may present with bilateral rather than unilateral headache and shorter attack duration. Management includes paediatric-adapted preventive therapy and non-pharmacological approaches.

simpleName: Chronic migraine (chronic daily headache, transformed migraine)
symptomKeywords: migraine more than 15 days a month
metaDescription: Chronic migraine: 15+ headache days per month. CGRP antibodies, Botox, topiramate, and MOH withdrawal reviewed. Evidence-based prevention explained.
